content:
9 min read
Just now

--

The senior engineers shipping fastest with AI aren’t writing better prompts. They’re building feedback loops that prompt the model for them.
Here is an uncomfortable truth. If you are still typing one prompt, reading the answer, then typing the next prompt, you are doing the AI equivalent of hand cranking an engine. It works. It just isn’t where the leverage is anymore.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Boris Cherny, who leads Claude Code at Anthropic, put it plainly: “I don’t prompt Claude anymore. I have loops running that prompt Claude and figuring out what to do. My job is to write loops.” Peter Steinberger said roughly the same thing. The interesting part isn’t the quote. It is that the tooling finally caught up enough to make this real for the rest of us, not just for people sitting inside Anthropic.
I want to walk through what this actually looks like in practice, including a setup I have been running where three loop driven agents act like a tiny engineering team. I will also be honest about where it falls apart, because it does, and the failure modes are sneaky.
The problem: single-shot prompting hits a ceiling
Ask an AI to “refactor this module and fix the failing tests” in one prompt, and you are gambling on the model nailing it in a single pass. Sometimes it does. More often it gets you eighty percent of the way there, quietly introduces a regression somewhere else, and then tells you with total confidence that everything is done.
The core failure is that you made the maker also the grader. The same model that wrote the code is the one deciding whether the code is good. That is like asking a student to grade their own exam and then walking out of the room.
The reason this matters more as models get better is subtle. A weaker model fails loudly and you catch it. A stronger model fails quietly and convincingly, which is worse, because you stop checking.
The solution in one sentence
Loop engineering means you stop expecting a one shot answer and instead design a system that generates, evaluates, revises, and repeats until the output meets a standard you defined up front.
You write the loop once. The loop does the prompting. You move up a level and become the person who designs the process, not the person babysitting the tool one message at a time.
The mental model: a thermostat, not a microwave
A microwave is open loop. You set ninety seconds, it runs blindly, and if your food comes out cold in the middle, too bad, it never knew. A thermostat is closed loop. It measures the room, compares it to a target, nudges the heat, then measures again, forever.
Single shot prompting is a microwave. Loop engineering is a thermostat. The clever part was never the heating element, which is the model. It is the feedback path that keeps checking reality against a goal.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A concrete walkthrough
Let us make it real. Say you want the AI to write a function that passes a test suite. Here is the loop, stripped down to its skeleton in Python:
def loop_engineer(task, max_iterations=5):
code = generate(task) # 1. Generate
for i in range(max_iterations):
result = run_tests(code) # 2. Evaluate (objective signal)
if result.passed:
return code # standard met, done
# 3. Revise using the actual failure as feedback
code = generate(
task,
previous=code,
feedback=result.error_log # this is the closed loop
)
raise LoopExceeded("never met the bar")Notice what is doing the judging. It is run_tests(), not the model. The stopping condition is verifiable and lives outside the model's opinion of itself. That one design choice is the whole difference between a real loop and a chatbot that says "looks good to me" and moves on.
The non-negotiable: keep the maker away from the checker
When you cannot use a hard signal like tests, use a second agent with different instructions, ideally a different model, to grade the first one’s work. Claude Code’s /goal command does exactly this under the hood. A fresh model decides whether the loop is finished, not the one that wrote the code. Codex ships the same primitive. The split sounds bureaucratic until the first time a reviewer agent catches a bug the builder talked itself past.
The fun part: standing up a loop driven agent team
Once you have a working loop, the obvious next thought hits you. If one loop can build and check a function, why not wire up several loops that behave like a small product team? This is where it gets genuinely interesting, and also where most people quietly set fire to their token budget.
Here is the team I have been running. Three roles, each its own agent with its own instructions, each on its own loop, all coordinating through a shared markdown file on disk that acts as the team’s memory.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The builder picks the top open task off the board, writes code in an isolated git worktree so it cannot collide with anyone else, and runs the tests until they pass. The reviewer runs on a different model with a deliberately skeptical prompt. Its only job is to find reasons the change is wrong, not to be agreeable. The integrator takes approved work, rebases it, runs the full suite one more time, and opens the pull request.
A nightly automation kicks the whole thing off by reading yesterday’s CI failures and open issues into the board. By morning there is a stack of pull requests waiting, each one already reviewed by a second agent and gated behind real tests. The first time it ran end to end without me touching a key, it honestly felt like cheating.
What this is great at

• Boring, well specified work. Dependency bumps, flaky test triage, migrating a pattern across forty files. Tasks where “done” is obvious and the judgment bar is low.

• Parallelism you could never staff. Five worktrees, five builders, all running while you sleep. No human team scales like that on trivial work.

• Catching the dumb stuff. The reviewer agent reliably flags missing null checks, forgotten error handling, and tests that pass for the wrong reason.
Now the drawbacks, because they are real
I do not want to sell you a fantasy. A team of agents amplifies your judgment, and it amplifies your mistakes just as fast. Here is what bit me.

• Token cost compounds, it does not add. Three agents, each looping three or four times, each re-reading context every cycle, turns into ten or fifteen model calls for one small change. My bill for an “automated” weekend was not small. Loops are cheap to start and expensive to leave running.

• The reviewer and builder can collude. If both run the same model family, they share the same blind spots. The reviewer waves through the exact mistake the builder loves to make. You think you have a check. You have an echo with extra steps.

• Plausible nonsense passes. An agent team is very good at producing code that looks reviewed, looks tested, and looks done. When all three signals are themselves generated, “looks done” and “is done” drift apart, and you find out in production.

• Your understanding rots quietly. The faster the team ships code you did not write, the larger the gap between what the repo does and what you actually grasp. A smooth loop just grows that gap faster, unless you force yourself to read what it produced.

• Coordination bugs are nasty. Two builders editing related files in separate worktrees both pass their own tests, then break each other on merge. Worktrees stop the file collision, not the logical one.

• You are still the bottleneck. The team can open twenty pull requests overnight. It cannot care about your product. If you rubber stamp them to keep up, the whole point of review just evaporated.
The honest rule I landed on: let the agent team do the work, but never let it own the decision to ship. Generation can be automated. Accountability cannot.
Does it actually pay off? The numbers
Iterative self-correction is not folklore. The Reflexion paper (Shinn et al., 2023) showed agents that reflect on failures and retry hit 91% pass@1 on HumanEval, versus 80% for GPT-4 alone. Self-Debugging (Chen et al., 2023) reported accuracy jumps of up to 12 points simply by letting the model read its own execution traces and retry. MetaGPT (Hong et al., 2023) pushed the team idea further, assigning role based agents to a shared workflow and reporting stronger results than single agent baselines on multi step tasks.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Read that cost column twice. More iterations and more agents burn more tokens, and the team setup is the most expensive thing on the list. Spend that budget where a second and third opinion is genuinely worth paying for, not on a regex tweak you could have done in ten seconds.
Common mistakes

• No exit condition. A loop without a hard stop is a token furnace. Always cap iterations and define a measurable “done.”

• Letting the model grade itself. If maker and checker are the same model with the same prompt, you do not have a loop, you have an echo.

• Vague success criteria. “Make it better” never terminates. “All tests in test/auth pass and lint is clean" does.

• Forgetting state. Models forget everything between runs. Without a markdown file or ticket board on disk, every iteration re-derives your project from zero.

• Scaling the team before the loop is solid. Three flaky agents are not better than one reliable one, they are three times the chaos. Get a single loop trustworthy first, then add roles.
Pro tips from the trenches

• Make your stop condition machine-checkable. Tests, a linter, a schema validator, anything that returns true or false beats a vibe check.

• Log every iteration’s feedback. When a loop misbehaves, the failure trail tells you whether your prompt or your evaluator is broken.

• Start with two iterations, not ten. Most quality gains land in the first revision. Tune up only if the data justifies it.

• Write project knowledge into a skill file once. A SKILL.md on disk stops the loop from re-guessing your conventions every cycle.

• Mix your models on purpose. Put a different model on the reviewer than the builder. Shared blind spots are the quietest way a team loop fails.
The practical summary

• Do not expect one prompt to nail a task. Design it to generate, evaluate, revise, then repeat.

• Make evaluation external and objective, using tests, linters, or a separate verifier agent.

• Define a verifiable stop condition and a hard cap on iterations.

• Persist state outside the conversation so the loop compounds instead of resetting.

• Budget tokens deliberately. Quality scales with cost, so spend where it actually matters.

• Scale to a team only after a single loop is reliable, and keep the ship decision human.

The shift here is subtle but it is big. Prompt engineering optimized the input. Loop engineering optimizes the entire feedback system around the model, and that is where the durable leverage lives. Two engineers can build the identical loop, or the identical agent team, and get opposite results. The loop does not know whether you are using it to move faster on work you understand deeply, or to avoid understanding the work at all. It cannot tell the difference. You can.
That is exactly what makes loop design harder than prompt engineering, not easier. The work did not get simpler. The leverage point just moved, and it moved toward the people willing to stay responsible for what ships.
So build the loop. Stand up the team. Let it run while you sleep. Just build it like someone who intends to stay the engineer, not just the person who presses go.
Now your turn. What is the first repetitive task you would hand to a loop tomorrow, and what would you absolutely never let it ship without you? Drop both in the comments. If this reframed how you think about working with AI, give it a clap so it reaches the next engineer still hand cranking prompts.
